questions.text: Food security means:
questions.explanation: Food security has three dimensions: Availability, Accessibility, and Affordability to all people at all times.

questions.text: 'Availability of food' means:
options: Food production within the country plus imports and stock
questions.explanation: It refers to the overall supply of food grains within the country.

questions.text: 'Accessibility' means food is:
options: Within reach of every person without barrier
questions.explanation: Accessibility ensures that everyone can physically get to the food.

questions.text: 'Affordability' implies that an individual has enough money to buy:
questions.explanation: It means the person has the financial means to meet their dietary needs.

questions.text: Who are the most food-insecure people in India?
options: Landless people with little or no land
questions.explanation: These groups lack stable income and resources to ensure regular food supply.

questions.text: During a natural calamity (like drought), total production of food grains:
questions.explanation: Calamities lead to a shortage of food in affected areas, causing prices to rise.

questions.text: A massive starvation can lead to a:
questions.explanation: A famine is characterized by widespread deaths due to starvation and epidemics caused by forced use of contaminated water.

questions.text: The most devastating famine that occurred in India in 1943 was the:
questions.explanation: The Bengal famine killed thirty lakh people in the province of Bengal.

questions.text: Which of the following social groups is more prone to food insecurity?
questions.explanation: Socially disadvantaged groups often have poor land-bases or low productivity, making them vulnerable.

questions.text: People who migrate to other areas in search of work are:
questions.explanation: Migration is often a desperate measure taken by those who cannot find food or work in their home villages.

questions.text: Hunger is not just an expression of poverty; it ________ poverty.
questions.explanation: Chronic hunger leads to poor health and inability to work, which traps people in poverty.

questions.text: ________ hunger is a consequence of diets persistently inadequate in terms of quantity and quality.
questions.explanation: Chronic hunger occurs when people are unable to buy enough food even for survival.

questions.text: ________ hunger is related to cycles of food growing and harvesting.
questions.explanation: It is common in rural areas during months when there is no farm work.

questions.text: India aimed at self-sufficiency in food grains since Independence by adopting the:
questions.explanation: Green Revolution focused on high-yielding varieties to increase grain production.

questions.text: In 1968, Indira Gandhi released a special stamp entitled ________ to record the success of the Green Revolution.
questions.explanation: The success was most visible in wheat production initially.

questions.text: The two main components of the food security system in India are:
options: Buffer Stock and Public Distribution System
questions.explanation: These two components ensure the availability and distribution of food at affordable prices.

questions.text: Buffer Stock is the stock of foodgrains procured by the government through:
questions.explanation: FCI buys wheat and rice from farmers in states where there is surplus production.

questions.text: The price paid to farmers for their crops by the government is called:
questions.explanation: MSP is announced before the sowing season to provide incentives to farmers.

questions.text: The price at which food grains are distributed to the poorer sections of society is:
questions.explanation: Issue price is lower than the market price and is subsidized by the government.

questions.text: The system of distributing food through government-regulated shops is called:
questions.explanation: PDS shops are also known as Ration Shops.

questions.text: Ration shops keep stock of:
questions.explanation: These shops provide essential items to people with ration cards at lower prices.

questions.text: Which type of Ration card is for the 'poorest of the poor'?
questions.explanation: AAY cards provide the highest subsidy on food grains.

questions.text: The introduction of Rationing in India dates back to the:
questions.explanation: Rationing was first introduced against the backdrop of the Bengal famine.

questions.text: What does ICDS stand for?
questions.explanation: ICDS was introduced in the mid-1970s to provide food and healthcare to children and mothers.

questions.text: Under the National Food Security Act (2013), ________ of rural and ________ of urban population are entitled to subsidized food grains.
questions.explanation: This act legalized the right to food for a large part of the population.

questions.text: The subsidy is a payment that the government makes to:
options: A producer or supplier to keep the market price low
questions.explanation: Government covers part of the cost to make items affordable for the poor.

questions.text: FCI stores foodgrains in:
questions.explanation: Proper storage in granaries prevents wastage and damage by pests.

questions.text: Which state has a successful 'Grain Bank' program facilitated by NGOs?
questions.explanation: NGOs like Academy of Development Science (ADS) have set up grain banks in Maharashtra.

questions.text: In Tamil Nadu, nearly ________ of all fair price shops are run by cooperatives.
questions.explanation: Cooperatives play a major role in food distribution in Tamil Nadu.

questions.text: Mother Dairy in Delhi is a success story of:
questions.explanation: Mother Dairy provides milk and vegetables at controlled prices to consumers.

questions.text: Who was the 'Father of the Green Revolution' in the world?
questions.explanation: Norman Borlaug introduced the high-yielding varieties globally.

questions.text: Excessive Buffer Stock is also a problem because:
options: It leads to storage rot and wastage
questions.explanation: Holding too much grain can be wasteful and costly if not managed well.

questions.text: Revamped Public Distribution System (RPDS) was launched in 1992 to target:
questions.explanation: RPDS was aimed at providing benefits to remote and backward areas.

questions.text: TPDS (Targeted Public Distribution System) introduced in 1997 focused on:
questions.explanation: TPDS adopted the principle of targeting the 'poor' specifically (BPL).

questions.text: What is one criticism of PDS?
options: Grains are sometimes of poor quality
questions.explanation: Sometimes PDS dealers divert grain to open markets or sell inferior quality food.

questions.text: Self-sufficiency in food grains has helped India avoid ________ even during adverse weather.
questions.explanation: A robust buffer stock ensures that food is available even during crop failures.

questions.text: The Academy of Development Science (ADS) is located in:
questions.explanation: ADS has been active in organizing grain banks in Maharashtra.

questions.text: Which grain has the highest production increase during the Green Revolution?
questions.explanation: Wheat saw a spectacular growth, often termed as the 'Wheat Revolution'.

questions.text: The Food Corporation of India (FCI) was set up to:
options: Procure and store foodgrains for food security
questions.explanation: FCI is the nodal agency for managing food stocks in India.

questions.text: Food security is necessary for:
questions.explanation: Every nation needs to ensure its citizens have reliable access to food for health and stability.
